content:
# Luke Chapter 19 — The EMET Bible (Literal)
**Translator:** EMET | **Source:** Codex Sinaiticus (Aleph, 01), PDF pages 111-113 | **Source Image:** source-texts/greek-NT/LUK_019_page_001.jpg, LUK_019_page_002.jpg, LUK_019_page_003.jpg

---

**1.** And having entered, he was passing-through Jericho.

**2.** And behold, a man by name being-called Zacchaeus, and he was a chief-tax-collector, and he [was] wealthy.

**3.** And he was seeking to see Jesus, who he is, and was not able because-of the crowd, because he was small in stature.

**4.** And having run ahead to the [place] before, he climbed-up upon a sycamore-tree, so-that he might see him, because he was about to pass-through that [way].

**5.** And as he came to the place, Jesus, having looked-up, said to him, "Zacchaeus, having hurried, come-down; for today I must stay in your house."

**6.** And having hurried, he came-down, and received him rejoicing.

**7.** And having seen [this], all were grumbling, saying that, "He went-in with a sinful man to lodge."

**8.** And having stood, Zacchaeus said to the Lord, "Behold, the half of my possessions, Lord, I give to the poor; and if I have extorted-from anyone anything, I give-back fourfold."

**9.** And Jesus said to him, "Today salvation has come-to-be to this house, inasmuch-as he also is a son of Abraham.

**10.** For the Son of Man came to seek and to save the [one] having been lost."

**11.** And [while] they were hearing these [things], having added [to this] he spoke a parable, because of his being near to Jerusalem, and [because] they were thinking that the kingdom of God is about to appear immediately.

**12.** Therefore he said, "A certain man of-noble-birth traveled to a far country to receive for himself a kingdom and to return.

**13.** And having called ten servants of his-own, he gave them ten minas, and said to them, 'Do-business [with these] while I am coming [back].'

**14.** But his citizens were hating him, and sent-out a delegation after him, saying, 'We do not want this [man] to reign over us.'

**15.** And it came-to-be, upon his returning, having received the kingdom, he also ordered these servants to be called to him, to whom he had given the silver, that he might know what they had gained-by-trading.

**16.** And the first came-before [him], saying, 'Lord, your mina has earned ten minas.'

**17.** And he said to him, 'Well [done], good servant! Because you were faithful in [a] very-small [thing], be having authority over ten cities.'

**18.** And the second came, saying, 'Your mina, Lord, has made five minas.'

**19.** And he said also to this [one], 'And you, be over five cities.'

**20.** And the other came, saying, 'Lord, behold, your mina, which I had lying-away in a face-cloth.

**21.** For I was fearing you, because you are a harsh man; you take-up what you did not put-down, and you reap what you did not sow.'

**22.** He says to him, 'Out-of your [own] mouth I will judge you, wicked servant. You knew that I am a harsh man, taking-up what I did not put-down, and reaping what I did not sow?

**23.** And why did you not give my silver to [the] bank, and I, having come, would have collected it with interest?'

**24.** And to those standing-by he said, 'Take from him the mina, and give [it] to the [one] having the ten minas.'

**25.** And they said to him, 'Lord, he has ten minas!'

**26.** 'I say to you that to everyone who has, [more] will be given; but from the [one] who does not have, even what he has will be taken-away.

**27.** Moreover, those enemies of mine, those not wanting me to reign over them — bring them here and slay [them] before me.'"

**28.** And having said these [things], he was going-on ahead, going-up to Jerusalem.

**29.** And it came-to-be, as he drew-near to Bethphage and Bethany, toward the mountain being called [the Mount] of Olives, he sent-out two of the disciples,

**30.** saying, "Go into the village opposite, in which, entering, you will find a colt tied, upon which no-one of men has ever sat; [and] having loosed [it], bring [it].

**31.** And if anyone asks you, 'Why are you loosing [it]?' thus you shall say, 'The Lord has need of it.'"

**32.** And having departed, those who had been sent found [it] just-as he said to them.

**33.** And [while] they were loosing the colt, its owners said to them, "Why are you loosing the colt?"

**34.** And they said, "The Lord has need of it."

**35.** And they brought it to Jesus, and having thrown their garments upon the colt, they set Jesus-upon [it].

**36.** And [as] he was going, they were spreading-under their garments on the road.

**37.** And [as] he was drawing-near already at the descent of the Mount of Olives, the whole multitude of the disciples began rejoicing to praise God [with] a loud voice concerning all the works-of-power [which] they had seen,

**38.** saying, "Blessed [is] the King, the [one] coming in [the] name of [the] Lord! Peace in heaven and glory in [the] highest [places]!"

**39.** And some of the Pharisees from the crowd said to him, "Teacher, rebuke your disciples."

**40.** And having answered, he said, "I say to you, if these shall be-silent, the stones will cry-out."

**41.** And as he drew-near, having seen the city, he wept over it,

**42.** saying, "If you had known in this day, even you, the [things leading] to peace — but now they were hidden from your eyes.

**43.** Because days will come upon you, and your enemies will throw-up a palisade against you, and will surround you, and will press-you-in from-every-side,

**44.** and will level-you-to-the-ground, and your children within you, and they will not leave stone upon stone in you, because you did not know the time of your visitation."

**45.** And having entered into the temple, he began to cast-out those selling,

**46.** saying to them, "It has been written, 'And my house shall be a house of prayer,' but you have made it a den of robbers."

**47.** And he was teaching daily in the temple. But the chief-priests and the scribes were seeking to destroy him, and the chief-ones of the people,

**48.** and they were not finding what they might do, for all the people were hanging-upon [him], listening.

---

## EMET's Textual Notes

1. **19:1-2 — "a chief-tax-collector" (αρχιτελωνης):** Sinaiticus reads και ιδου ανηρ ονοματι καλουμενος Ζακχαιος και αυτος ην αρχιτελωνης και αυτος πλουσιος. The word αρχιτελωνης ("chief-tax-collector") is a hapax legomenon in the entire Greek Bible — it appears nowhere else in the NT, LXX, or classical literature. It is a compound of αρχι- ("chief/ruling") + τελωνης ("tax-collector"), indicating Zacchaeus was not merely a tax-collector but the supervisor of tax-collectors in the Jericho district. Jericho was a major customs hub: the city controlled the lucrative balsam trade and the Jordan River crossing point for commerce. The double use of και αυτος ("and he") — "and he was a chief-tax-collector, and he [was] wealthy" — is emphatic, stacking these two facts as if in accusation. The name Ζακχαιος is Hellenized from the Hebrew זַכַּי (zakkai, "pure/innocent") — a deeply ironic name for a man the crowd considers a sinner (v. 7). Luke 18 ended with a blind man crying out; Luke 19 opens with a rich man climbing a tree. Both are desperate to encounter Jesus, but the contrast is deliberate: the blind beggar had nothing; Zacchaeus has everything except what matters.

2. **19:3-4 — "small in stature" (τη ηλικια μικρος):** Sinaiticus reads τη ηλικια μικρος ην — the word ηλικια can mean either "age" or "stature/height," but in context the sycamore-climbing makes clear it refers to physical shortness. The συκομορεαν ("sycamore-tree," from συκον "fig" + μορον "mulberry") is the ficus sycomorus, a sturdy, wide-branching tree with low branches easy to climb — common along roads in the Jordan valley. The participle προδραμων ("having run ahead") indicates Zacchaeus ran ahead of the crowd to a strategic vantage point — a dignified man abandoning dignity, foreshadowing the father who runs in 15:20. The verb αναβαινω ("to climb-up") is the same verb used for "going up" to Jerusalem and to the temple — Zacchaeus's ascent into a tree is a comic counterpoint to the sacred ascents of the narrative.

3. **19:5 — "I must stay in your house" (εν τω οικω σου δει με μειναι):** Sinaiticus reads σημερον γαρ εν τω οικω σου δει με μειναι. The verb δει ("it is necessary/I must") expresses divine necessity — the same word used for the passion predictions ("the Son of Man must suffer," 9:22; 17:25). Jesus' visit to Zacchaeus's house is not social convenience but divine appointment. The adverb σημερον ("today") is a Lukan keyword of salvation-immediacy (cf. 2:11 "today a Savior is born"; 4:21 "today this Scripture is fulfilled"; 23:43 "today you will be with me in paradise"). The verb μειναι ("to stay/remain") indicates more than a brief visit — it implies lodging, table fellowship, extended presence. Jesus does not wait for Zacchaeus to reform; he invites himself into the sinner's house before any repentance is declared.

4. **19:7 — "He went-in with a sinful man to lodge" (παρα αμαρτωλω ανδρι εισηλθεν καταλυσαι):** Sinaiticus reads παρα αμαρτωλω ανδρι εισηλθεν καταλυσαι. The verb καταλυω ("to lodge/to find lodging") literally means "to loose-down/to unyoke" — as travelers unyoked their animals at an inn. The word παντες ("all") indicates that the grumbling was universal — not just the Pharisees but the entire crowd, including the disciples. The phrase αμαρτωλω ανδρι ("a sinful man") places the adjective before the noun for emphasis — "a sinful man" rather than "a man who is sinful." In the eyes of the crowd, Zacchaeus's profession made him categorically unclean — a collaborator with Rome, ritually defiled by constant contact with Gentiles, and economically parasitic on his own people.

5. **19:8 — "I give to the poor...I give-back fourfold" (τοις πτωχοις διδωμι...αποδιδωμι τετραπλουν):** Sinaiticus reads σταθεις δε Ζακχαιος ειπεν προς τον κυριον· ιδου τα ημισια μου των υπαρχοντων κυριε τοις πτωχοις διδωμι· και ει τινος τι εσυκοφαντησα αποδιδωμι τετραπλουν. The verbs διδωμι ("I give") and αποδιδωμι ("I give-back") are present tense — a critical interpretive question: is Zacchaeus describing his current practice ("I [habitually] give...I [habitually] restore") or declaring a new resolution ("I [hereby] give...I [hereby] restore")? If present habitual, Jesus vindicates a man already generous; if present-for-future (a performative declaration), Zacchaeus announces radical transformation. The context (the crowd's accusation, Jesus' response about salvation "coming" to the house) favors the latter: this is conversion speech. The verb εσυκοφαντησα ("I have extorted-from") is the same verb used by the soldiers in 3:14 (μηδενα διασεισητε μηδε συκοφαντησητε, "extort no one") — literally "to show figs," from the alleged practice of informing on illegal fig-exporters in Athens, hence "to make false accusations for profit." Zacchaeus's fourfold restoration (τετραπλουν) exceeds the Torah requirement: Exodus 22:1 requires fourfold for stolen sheep but only double (διπλουν) for most theft (Exodus 22:4, 7). He imposes on himself the harshest restitution standard, treating his extortion as the equivalent of slaughtering what was stolen.

6. **19:9-10 — "Today salvation has come-to-be to this house" (σημερον σωτηρια τω οικω τουτω εγενετο):** Sinaiticus reads σημερον σωτηρια τω οικω τουτω εγενετο καθοτι και αυτος υιος Αβρααμ εστιν. The word σωτηρια ("salvation") is here personified — salvation "came" (εγενετο) to the house, but Jesus is the one who entered the house. Jesus himself is the σωτηρια. The phrase τω οικω τουτω ("to this house") extends salvation beyond Zacchaeus alone to his entire household. The reason given — καθοτι και αυτος υιος Αβρααμ εστιν ("inasmuch-as he also is a son of Abraham") — is stunning: the crowd called him "a sinful man," but Jesus calls him "a son of Abraham." The conjunction και ("also/even") is emphatic: even he, the chief-tax-collector, remains a son of Abraham. Verse 10 — ηλθεν γαρ ο υιος του ανθρωπου ζητησαι και σωσαι το απολωλος ("for the Son of Man came to seek and to save the [one] having been lost") — is the programmatic statement of Jesus' entire mission in Luke. The perfect participle απολωλος (from απολλυμι, "to destroy/to lose") indicates a state of being lost that still obtains — the lost one is still lost until found. The two infinitives ζητησαι ("to seek") and σωσαι ("to save") indicate two stages: God does not wait to be found but actively searches, and then rescues.

7. **19:11-12 — The parable of the minas:** Sinaiticus reads ακουοντων δε αυτων ταυτα προσθεις ειπεν παραβολην δια το εγγυς ειναι Ιερουσαλημ αυτον και δοκειν αυτους οτι παραχρημα μελλει η βασιλεια του θεου αναφαινεσθαι. Luke alone provides this double motivation for the parable: (1) proximity to Jerusalem — Jesus is nearing the capital where expectations will reach fever pitch; (2) the crowd's expectation that the kingdom would "appear immediately" (παραχρημα...αναφαινεσθαι). The verb αναφαινω ("to appear/to become-visible") is used of celestial phenomena — the kingdom was expected to blaze forth like a star. The parable corrects this by introducing delay, absence, and interim responsibility. The "man of-noble-birth" (ανθρωπος τις ευγενης) traveling to receive a kingdom mirrors the historical situation of Archelaus, Herod's son, who traveled to Rome in 4 BC to receive confirmation of his kingship over Judea — and whose citizens sent a delegation to oppose him (cf. v. 14). Jesus' audience would have recognized the political parallel immediately.

8. **19:13 — "ten minas" (δεκα μνας):** Sinaiticus reads εδωκεν αυτοις δεκα μνας. A μνα (mina) was worth 100 drachmas (roughly 100 days' wages for a laborer) — a significant but not enormous sum. Unlike the parable of the talents (Matthew 25:14-30), where different servants receive different amounts (5, 2, and 1 talent — and a talent was worth 6,000 drachmas), here each servant receives an identical amount: one mina each. The equality of the gift makes the inequality of the results entirely a matter of the servants' diligence. The command πραγματευσασθε εν ω ερχομαι ("do-business [with these] while I am coming [back]") uses the aorist imperative of πραγματευομαι ("to conduct business/to trade") — found only here in the NT — followed by εν ω ερχομαι ("in which [time] I am coming"), a present tense indicating the certainty of the master's return.

9. **19:14 — "We do not want this [man] to reign over us" (ου θελομεν τουτον βασιλευσαι εφ ημας):** Sinaiticus reads οι δε πολιται αυτου εμισουν αυτον και απεστειλαν πρεσβειαν οπισω αυτου λεγοντες· ου θελομεν τουτον βασιλευσαι εφ ημας. The verb εμισουν ("were hating," imperfect) indicates ongoing, settled hatred. The πρεσβεια ("delegation/embassy") sent "after him" (οπισω αυτου) to the distant country where he seeks his kingdom corresponds to the historical embassy of fifty Jewish aristocrats who went to Rome to oppose Archelaus's appointment (Josephus, Antiquities 17.300-314). In the parable's allegory, the "citizens" represent Israel's rejection of Jesus' messianic kingship. The demonstrative τουτον ("this [man]") is contemptuous — "this fellow."

10. **19:15-19 — The accounting:** Sinaiticus reads the first servant's report: η μνα σου κυριε δεκα προσηργασατο μνας ("your mina, Lord, earned ten minas") — a tenfold return, extraordinary business success. The verb προσεργαζομαι ("to earn in addition/to gain by trading") appears only here in the NT. The reward is governance: ισθι εξουσιαν εχων επανω δεκα πολεων ("be having authority over ten cities") — the faithful servant receives authority proportional to his faithfulness, not proportional to the original gift. The second servant earned five minas and receives five cities. The proportionality is exact: one mina produces ten, authority over ten; one mina produces five, authority over five. The phrase ευγε αγαθε δουλε ("well [done], good servant") uses ευγε — an exclamation of praise found only here and in a variant of Matthew 25:21.

11. **19:20-21 — "I had [it] lying-away in a face-cloth" (ειχον αποκειμενην εν σουδαριω):** Sinaiticus reads ην ειχον αποκειμενην εν σουδαριω. The σουδαριον ("face-cloth/sweat-cloth") is a Latin loanword (sudarium) — a small cloth used for wiping perspiration, later used for wrapping the face of the dead (cf. John 11:44; 20:7). Storing a mina in a σουδαριον is an image of paralysis: the servant neither invested nor spent but simply preserved the coin in a cloth, accomplishing nothing. His excuse — εφοβουμην σε ("I was fearing you") — uses the imperfect, indicating habitual, ongoing fear. He characterizes the master as αυστηρος ("harsh/strict") — literally "dry/astringent" (from αυω, "to dry") — a word used for unripe fruit that puckers the mouth. His accusations — αιρεις ο ουκ εθηκας ("you take-up what you did not put-down") and θεριζεις ο ουκ εσπειρας ("you reap what you did not sow") — portray the master as an exploiter who profits from others' labor.

12. **19:22-23 — "Out-of your [own] mouth I will judge you" (εκ του στοματος σου κρινω σε):** Sinaiticus reads εκ του στοματος σου κρινω σε πονηρε δουλε. The master does not dispute the servant's characterization but uses it as the basis for condemnation: if you knew (ηδεις, pluperfect — "you had known") I was harsh, then your inaction is inexcusable — you should have at least put the money on the τραπεζαν ("table/bank" — literally a money-changer's table) to earn τοκος ("interest" — literally "offspring," from τικτω, "to give birth" — money that breeds money). The question δια τι ("why?") demands justification that cannot be given. The servant's fear produced not obedience but paralysis — the opposite of what even fear should have motivated.

13. **19:24-26 — "Take from him the mina" (αρατε απ αυτου την μναν):** Sinaiticus reads αρατε απ αυτου την μναν και δοτε τω τας δεκα μνας εχοντι. The bystanders' protest — κυριε εχει δεκα μνας ("Lord, he has ten minas!") — reveals the human sense of fairness: why give more to the one who already has the most? The master's response articulates a kingdom principle: τω παντι εχοντι δοθησεται ("to everyone who has, [more] will be given") — faithfulness attracts more responsibility and more reward; απο δε του μη εχοντος και ο εχει αρθησεται ("but from the [one] who does not have, even what he has will be taken-away") — unfaithfulness leads to total loss. This is not about material wealth but about the stewardship of what has been entrusted: use it or lose it.

14. **19:27 — "slay [them] before me" (κατασφαξατε αυτους εμπροσθεν μου):** Sinaiticus reads πλην τους εχθρους μου τουτους τους μη θελησαντας με βασιλευσαι επ αυτους αγαγετε ωδε και κατασφαξατε αυτους εμπροσθεν μου. The verb κατασφαζω ("to slaughter/to slay") is intensified by the prefix κατα- ("down/completely") — this is not mere execution but slaughter. The word is violent and deliberate. In the historical parallel, Archelaus did indeed exact terrible vengeance upon his return from Rome. In the parabolic allegory, this refers to the judgment that falls upon those who reject the King's reign — a prophecy that Jesus will elaborate in his weeping over Jerusalem (vv. 41-44). The conjunction πλην ("moreover/nevertheless") marks a sharp transition from the servants' accountability to the citizens' rebellion — two different failures, two different judgments.

15. **19:28-29 — "Bethphage and Bethany, toward the Mount of Olives":** Sinaiticus reads ως ηγγισεν εις Βηθφαγη και Βηθανιαν προς το ορος το καλουμενον Ελαιων. The name Βηθφαγη is from Aramaic בֵּית פָּגֵי (beth page, "house of unripe figs") and Βηθανια from בֵּית עַנְיָה (beth anya, "house of affliction" or "house of Ananiah"). Both villages sit on the Mount of Olives' slopes, east of Jerusalem. The Mount of Olives (το ορος το καλουμενον Ελαιων — "the mountain being called [the Mount] of Olives") carries immense prophetic weight: Zechariah 14:4 prophesies that the Lord will stand on the Mount of Olives in the day of judgment. Jesus' approach from this direction is a messianic claim enacted in geography.

16. **19:30 — "a colt tied, upon which no-one of men has ever sat" (πωλον δεδεμενον εφ ον ουδεις πωποτε ανθρωπων εκαθισεν):** Sinaiticus reads ευρησετε πωλον δεδεμενον εφ ον ουδεις πωποτε ανθρωπων εκαθισεν. The πωλος ("colt/young animal") in Luke is unspecified — it could be a young donkey or horse. The perfect passive participle δεδεμενον ("having been tied") indicates it stands bound and waiting. The phrase εφ ον ουδεις πωποτε ανθρωπων εκαθισεν ("upon which no-one of men has ever sat") marks the animal as consecrated — in the OT, animals used for sacred purposes must be unyoked and unused (Numbers 19:2; Deuteronomy 21:3; 1 Samuel 6:7). An animal never ridden is ritually pure, fit for the King's entry.

17. **19:31 — "The Lord has need of it" (ο κυριος αυτου χρειαν εχει):** Sinaiticus reads ο κυριος αυτου χρειαν εχει. The title ο κυριος ("the Lord") here is ambiguous — it could mean "its owner" (the colt's master) or "the Lord" (Jesus as κυριος). Luke's narrative suggests both simultaneously: Jesus is claiming the animal by sovereign right, and the owners recognize that right (v. 33-34). The verb χρειαν εχει ("has need") is startling applied to Jesus — the one who commands nature and heals the sick "has need" of a borrowed colt. The King enters his capital city on a borrowed animal, having no possessions of his own.

18. **19:37-38 — "Blessed [is] the King" (ευλογημενος ο βασιλευς):** Sinaiticus reads ευλογημενος ο ερχομενος ο βασιλευς εν ονοματι κυριου· ειρηνη εν ουρανω και δοξα εν υψιστοις. The crowd quotes Psalm 118:26 (LXX 117:26) — ευλογημενος ο ερχομενος εν ονοματι κυριου — but Luke alone inserts ο βασιλευς ("the King") into the citation, making the messianic identification explicit. The crowd's addition of ειρηνη εν ουρανω και δοξα εν υψιστοις ("peace in heaven and glory in [the] highest [places]") echoes the angels' song at Jesus' birth (2:14: δοξα εν υψιστοις θεω και επι γης ειρηνη) but with a crucial inversion: at the nativity it was "peace on earth"; now it is "peace in heaven." Peace on earth has been rejected (cf. vv. 42-44); peace now exists only in the heavenly realm. The descent of the Mount of Olives (τη καταβασει του ορους των Ελαιων) is the point where Jerusalem suddenly comes into full view across the Kidron Valley — the moment of visual impact that triggers the crowd's acclamation and, immediately after, Jesus' tears.

19. **19:39-40 — "the stones will cry-out" (οι λιθοι κραξουσιν):** Sinaiticus reads λεγω υμιν εαν ουτοι σιωπησουσιν οι λιθοι κραξουσιν. The Pharisees' demand — επιτιμησον τοις μαθηταις σου ("rebuke your disciples") — asks Jesus to silence the messianic acclamation. Jesus' response is that creation itself will testify if humans are silenced. The verb κραζω ("to cry-out/to scream") is used of raw, inarticulate cries — the stones would shriek. The saying may echo Habakkuk 2:11 (LXX: λιθος εκ τοιχου βοησεται, "a stone from the wall will cry-out") where the stones testify against injustice. If the King is denied human praise, inanimate creation will rupture its silence to provide it.

20. **19:41-42 — "he wept over it" (εκλαυσεν επ αυτην):** Sinaiticus reads και ως ηγγισεν ιδων την πολιν εκλαυσεν επ αυτην. The verb κλαιω ("to weep/to wail") indicates audible, visible weeping — not silent tears but lamentation. This is one of only two places in the Gospels where Jesus weeps (cf. John 11:35, εδακρυσεν, "he shed tears" — a milder word). The conditional sentence ει εγνως εν τη ημερα ταυτη και συ τα προς ειρηνην ("if you had known in this day, even you, the [things leading] to peace") is an unfulfilled condition (second class) broken off mid-sentence — the apodosis (the "then" clause) is never stated. The aposiopesis (deliberate breaking-off) conveys emotion too deep for completion. The emphatic και συ ("even you") is devastating: even you, Jerusalem, the city of peace (ירושלים, yeru-shalaim, "foundation of peace"), do not know what makes for peace. The phrase νυν δε εκρυβη απο οφθαλμων σου ("but now they were hidden from your eyes") uses the aorist passive εκρυβη — a divine passive indicating God has now hidden what was once available to see.

21. **19:43-44 — "days will come upon you" (ηξουσιν ημεραι επι σε):** Sinaiticus reads οτι ηξουσιν ημεραι επι σε και παρεμβαλουσιν οι εχθροι σου χαρακα σοι και περικυκλωσουσιν σε και συνεξουσιν σε παντοθεν. The vocabulary is precise military terminology: χαραξ ("palisade/siege-wall") — the Romans under Titus built a circumvallation wall around Jerusalem in 70 AD; περικυκλωσουσιν ("will surround") — complete encirclement; συνεξουσιν ("will press-you-in") from συνεχω ("to hold together/to compress") — the strangling siege. The verb εδαφιουσιν σε ("will level-you-to-the-ground"), from εδαφος ("ground/floor"), means to dash to the ground, to raze completely. The phrase και τα τεκνα σου εν σοι ("and your children within you") extends the destruction to the inhabitants — Jerusalem's children will be dashed along with her walls. The final clause — ανθ ων ουκ εγνως τον καιρον της επισκοπης σου ("because you did not know the time of your visitation") — gives the theological cause. The word επισκοπη ("visitation") derives from επισκεπτομαι ("to look-upon/to visit") — the same word used in Zechariah's song (1:68, 78) for God's redemptive visitation. The καιρος ("appointed time/critical moment") is not χρονος (ordinary time) but the decisive, unrepeatable moment of divine approach. Jerusalem's sin is not ignorance in the abstract but failure to recognize the moment when God came to her in the person of Jesus.

22. **19:45-46 — The temple cleansing:** Sinaiticus reads και εισελθων εις το ιερον ηρξατο εκβαλλειν τους πωλουντας. Luke's account is the briefest of the Synoptics — he omits the buyers, the money-changers' tables, and the dove-sellers' seats found in Mark 11:15-16 and Matthew 21:12. Only "those selling" (τους πωλουντας) are cast out. The combined citation draws from Isaiah 56:7 (εσται ο οικος μου οικος προσευχης, "my house shall be a house of prayer") and Jeremiah 7:11 (σπηλαιον ληστων, "den of robbers"). The word ληστης ("robber/bandit") is not κλεπτης ("thief/pilferer") — a ληστης uses violence; a κλεπτης uses stealth. The temple establishment is not accused of petty theft but of violent exploitation, turning the house of prayer into a fortified hideout for predators.

23. **19:47-48 — "hanging-upon [him], listening" (εξεκρεματο αυτου ακουων):** Sinaiticus reads ο λαος γαρ απας εξεκρεματο αυτου ακουων. The verb εκκρεμαμαι ("to hang-upon/to hang from") is a vivid image — the people hung on Jesus' words as one hangs from a cliff. This hapax legomenon in the NT (found only here) conveys absolute, riveted attention. The imperfect tense (εξεκρεματο) indicates this was the ongoing situation during Jesus' temple teaching. This popular devotion creates a protective shield around Jesus: the chief-priests, scribes, and "chief-ones of the people" (οι πρωτοι του λαου — the aristocratic leadership) were seeking (εζητουν, imperfect) to destroy him but could not find (ουχ ευρισκον, imperfect) a way because of the people's attachment. The tension between the leadership's murderous intent and the crowd's spellbound listening sets the stage for the passion narrative: betrayal by an insider (Judas) will be needed to break through the crowd's protective wall.

24. **19:1-48 — The structure of Luke 19:** The chapter moves from the private encounter in Jericho to the public entry into Jerusalem, from salvation coming to one house to judgment pronounced on an entire city. Zacchaeus receives Jesus joyfully (v. 6) and salvation comes (v. 9); Jerusalem fails to recognize its visitation and faces destruction (vv. 43-44). The parable of the minas bridges these two movements: it warns that the King's departure creates an interim of responsibility, that faithfulness will be rewarded with authority, that slothfulness will result in loss, and that rejection of the King's reign will result in slaughter. The chapter's emotional arc moves from joy (Zacchaeus), through warning (the parable), to triumphant praise (the entry), to inconsolable grief (the weeping), to righteous anger (the cleansing), and finally to tense stalemate (the temple teaching). Jesus enters Jerusalem as King, but a King who weeps for the city that will kill him.
